articles.title: Middle East Escalation: Diplomatic Talks Amid US-Iran Military Tensions
articles.summary: The US-Iran conflict continues with military strikes and diplomatic efforts underway. Trump administration officials have stated engagement with Iran on potential solutions, while Iran has threatened retaliation. China's foreign minister Wang Yi called for peace talks, emphasizing that issues should be resolved through dialogue rather than force.

articles.title: Sudan-Chad Border Crisis: Rapid Support Forces Cross-Border Attack
articles.summary: Chad has closed its border with Sudan following a cross-border attack by the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF). Military officials indicate Chadian army officers are increasingly acting outside formal command structures to support anti-RSF groups in Darfur, escalating regional security concerns.

articles.title: Gaza Ceasefire Status: Israel Resumes Airstrikes After Two-Month Pause
articles.summary: Israel launched air strikes on Gaza in early March, ending a two-month ceasefire. The UN is using this period to ramp up aid delivery to Gaza residents. Meanwhile, settler attacks have escalated dramatically against Palestinians in the occupied West Bank.

articles.title: Strait of Hormuz Closure Impacts Global Energy Markets
articles.summary: The closure of the Strait of Hormuz due to Middle East conflict has triggered significant economic consequences, with oil prices surging above $100 per barrel. The UN Security Council is negotiating a resolution to authorize states to use necessary means to ensure freedom of navigation in the critical waterway.

articles.title: DRC-Rwanda Tensions: AU-Led Mediation Efforts Continue Amid Conflict
articles.summary: Eastern DRC conflict escalates with M23 and other armed groups continuing violence in North Kivu and Ituri provinces. An AU panel led by Togolese President Faure Gnassingbé toured the region for consultations, while differing ceasefire agreements via Doha and Angola tracks complicate peace efforts.

articles.title: Ukraine Conflict: Fifth Year of War Tests International Resolve
articles.summary: Russia's invasion continues to reshape European security and strain Western alliances. As the conflict enters its fifth year, settlement terms remain as contested as the front lines themselves, with limited progress on diplomatic solutions.

articles.title: Advanced Machine Intelligence Labs Raises $1.03 Billion in Record European Seed Round
articles.summary: Advanced Machine Intelligence (AMI) Labs, founded by Turing Award winner Yann LeCun, has raised $1.03 billion in seed funding at a $3.5 billion valuation. The Paris-based startup is building 'world models,' an alternative AI architecture to large language models that learns by understanding how the physical world works, targeting applications in robotics, healthcare, and manufacturing.

articles.title: OpenAI Plans to Nearly Double Workforce to 8,000 Employees by End of 2026
articles.summary: OpenAI plans to nearly double its workforce to about 8,000 employees by the end of 2026 as it pushes harder into enterprise sales and product delivery. The company is hiring across product, engineering, research, sales, and 'technical ambassadorship,' while also expanding office space.

articles.title: Tailscale Acquires Border0 to Address AI Agent Security Challenges
articles.summary: Canadian cybersecurity startup Tailscale has acquired Border0, adding technology designed to help companies manage the growing wave of AI agents entering corporate systems. The move addresses the new reality that AI agents are becoming active participants on enterprise networks and servers.

articles.title: Elon Musk Announces Terafab, a $20 Billion Semiconductor Fabrication Facility
articles.summary: Elon Musk announced Terafab, a joint $20 billion-plus semiconductor fabrication facility in Austin, Texas, developed by Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI. The plant will manufacture custom chips optimized for electric vehicles, Optimus humanoid robots, and high-performance AI computing.

articles.title: Micron Reports Strong Q2 Sales Driven by AI Memory Demand
articles.summary: Micron forecast stronger-than-expected third-quarter revenue after reporting a sharp jump in second-quarter sales, fueled by booming demand for memory used in AI systems. Revenue hit $23.86 billion, beating expectations, while the company said it would increase 2026 capital spending by $5 billion to more than $25 billion. Micron is one of only three major global suppliers of high-bandwidth memory, making it critical to the AI supply chain.

articles.title: Russian Intelligence Services Targeting WhatsApp and Signal in Global Phishing Campaign
articles.summary: Threat actors affiliated with Russian Intelligence Services are conducting phishing campaigns to compromise commercial messaging applications like WhatsApp and Signal. The campaign targets individuals of high intelligence value, including current and former U.S. government officials, military personnel, political figures, and journalists. Globally, this effort has resulted in unauthorized access to thousands of individual accounts.

articles.title: Microsoft Patches Six Zero-Day Vulnerabilities in Latest Security Update
articles.summary: Microsoft released updates to fix more than 50 security holes in its Windows operating systems and other software, including patches for six 'zero-day' vulnerabilities that attackers are already exploiting in the wild. CVE-2026-21510 is a security feature bypass vulnerability in Windows Shell where a single click on a malicious link can quietly bypass Windows protections and run attacker-controlled content without warning.

articles.title: Markets Rally on Trump's Iran De-escalation Comments
articles.summary: Stock futures rebounded following a historic 1,100-point "Trump Rally" after President Trump postponed planned strikes on Iranian energy infrastructure for five days, citing "productive conversations" aimed at reopening the Strait of Hormuz. The S&P 500 gained 1.2%, the Nasdaq Composite advanced 1.4%, and the Dow Jones rose 1.4% or 631 points to close at 46,208.47.

articles.title: NYSE Partners with Securitize on Blockchain-Based Trading Platform
articles.summary: The New York Stock Exchange is partnering with Securitize to build a blockchain-based platform for tokenized stocks and ETFs, with the proposed system enabling 24/7 trading, instant settlement, and funding via stablecoins—bypassing traditional clearinghouses. The key question for investors is whether "native" tokenized shares can deliver full shareholder rights—unlike many current offshore offerings that behave more like derivatives.

articles.title: Gilead Sciences Acquires Ouro Medicines for $2.18 Billion
articles.summary: Gilead Sciences announced a $2.18 billion deal to buy Ouro Medicines, aimed at securing assets to bolster its growing inflammation portfolio, with Ouro's key drug gamgertamig already in the FDA fast-track process. Gilead is described as a reasonably priced healthcare stock with product sales projected to approach or exceed $30 billion in 2026.

articles.title: Federal Reserve Holds Interest Rates Steady Amid Iran War Uncertainty
articles.summary: The Federal Reserve voted to hold its key interest rate steady, with the Federal Open Market Committee voting 11-1 to keep the benchmark federal funds rate anchored in a range between 3.5%-3.75% as policymakers navigate higher-than-expected inflation readings, mixed labor market signals, and Middle East conflict. The Committee noted that uncertainty about the economic outlook remains elevated, with implications of Middle East developments for the U.S. economy being uncertain.

articles.title: Elliott Investment Builds Multibillion-Dollar Stake in Synopsys
articles.summary: Elliott Investment Management has built up a multibillion-dollar stake in chip-design software maker Synopsys and intends to push the company to grow its software and services profitability. The stock rose 2.5% in early trading following the announcement.

articles.title: Tariffs Expected to Impact U.S. Consumer Prices in 2026
articles.summary: One of the most consequential developments was Trump's sweeping new tariff regime, which increased the effective tariff rate from 2.1 percent to an estimated 11.7 percent as of January 2026 through executive authority. The best evidence suggests that pass-through to consumers now exceeds 50 percent, though slower and less complete than the near-100 percent pass-through observed under tariffs from the first Trump administration, still representing a meaningful burden on households.
articles.sourceName: Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research

articles.title: U.S. Economic Growth Expected to Continue at 2.2% in 2026
articles.summary: A modest economic tailwind fueled by expansionary fiscal policies, rate cuts by the Federal Reserve, full expensing of capital investments, and deregulation is expected to push overall U.S. growth to an above-trend 2.2% in 2026, with economists reducing the probability of recession over the next 12 months to 30% from 40%. The push to build infrastructure for artificial intelligence is expected to be the primary driver of growth, while upper-end consumers are anticipated to maintain their spending.

articles.title: Customized CRISPR Gene Therapy Breakthrough: First Personalized Treatment for Genetic Disease
articles.summary: KJ Muldoon, a baby born with a rare genetic disease, became the first person to receive a customized CRISPR treatment, unlike the two CRISPR-based therapies approved to date which are one-size-fits-all and require cells to be removed, edited in a lab, and then reintroduced into the body. The treatment dramatically reduced the child's need for medication and proved that truly individualized medicine is within reach.

articles.title: FDA Approves First Menin Inhibitors for Acute Myeloid Leukemia Treatment
articles.summary: Two targeted therapies called menin inhibitors were recently approved for approximately 40% of AML cases. Dana-Farber researchers are developing novel protein degraders that could be useful for treatment of many forms of cancer, with trials underway such as giredestrant, a selective estrogen receptor degrader (SERD), for the treatment of some forms of breast cancer.

articles.title: States Sue Over Federal Vaccine Policy Changes; Measles Risk Rises
articles.summary: The revised childhood immunization schedule wasn't based on new science or expert consensus, but rather an ideological agenda, with critics arguing the actions flout decades of scientific research and harm public health. Stoking vaccine doubts leads to lower vaccination rates, which leads to more disease outbreaks—such as the hundreds of measles cases reported in 26 states over the past two months.

articles.title: Immigration Enforcement Threatens Healthcare Access and Workforce
articles.summary: Patients are afraid to show up to medical appointments, while doctors, nurses, and home health aids are being detained, deported or losing their visa status. Foreign-born health care workers—who make up an estimated 20% of the health care workforce—report living in a state of fear.

articles.title: GLP-1 Medications Expand Beyond Obesity to Heart Disease and Addiction Treatment
articles.summary: The top health trend for 2026 is the expansion of GLP-1 medications, which experts believe will increasingly be used for managing a wide range of chronic conditions beyond just obesity and Type 2 diabetes, such as heart health, kidney disease and even addiction treatment. Companies are developing pills for weight loss, with a version of Rybelsus gaining FDA approval specifically for weight loss on December 22.

articles.title: AI-Powered Wearables and Personalized Nutrition Reshape Wellness in 2026
articles.summary: Wearable technology offering real-time metabolic feedback, including watches, rings and continuous glucose monitors, are the most revolutionary health technology of the future and will be used in conjunction with artificial intelligence for hyper-personalized meal planning and stress management tips. 38% of expert panelists deemed AI-integrated wearable technology as a top trend for 2026.

articles.title: U.S. Set to Add Record 86 Gigawatts of Electric Capacity in 2026
articles.summary: U.S. power plant developers and operators plan to add 86 gigawatts (GW) of new utility-scale electric generating capacity to the U.S. power grid in 2026, a record if realized. Developers plan to add 24 GW of utility-scale battery storage to the grid this year, compared with a record 15 GW added in 2025. A new project, Tehuacana Creek 1 Solar and BESS, adding 837 megawatts in Texas, is the largest solar photovoltaic project expected to come online in 2026.

articles.title: Global Wind and Solar Capacity Reaches Record 814 GW in 2025
articles.summary: New data from global energy think tank Ember shows that 814 gigawatts of new solar and wind capacity came online last year, up 17% from 2024's 696 GW, bringing total global wind and solar capacity to 4,174 GW. Solar made up the bulk of new additions in 2025, with nearly 4 GW of solar installed for every 1 GW of wind.

articles.title: U.S. Electricity Prices Surge 6.3% as AI Data Centers Strain Grid
articles.summary: Electricity prices surged 6.3% nationwide as AI data centers and winter weather strained the grid, outpacing inflation and hitting household budgets hard. Residential electricity prices rose by 11.5% in 2025, and prices are expected to increase by up to 40% by 2030 compared to 2025. Utilities requested more than $29 billion in rate increases in the first half of 2025, double the amount requested in the first half of 2024.

articles.title: Wind and Solar Generated Record 17% of U.S. Electricity in 2025
articles.summary: Over the past 20 years, electricity from wind power and utility-scale solar power has increased to 17% of generation in the United States compared to less than 1% in 2005, with net generation of wind and solar together accounting for 760,000 gigawatthours of electricity in 2025. Utility-scale solar power generation totaled 296,000 GWh, 34% more than in 2024.

articles.title: ERCOT Partners with McKinsey to Streamline Data Center Grid Interconnections
articles.summary: ERCOT and McKinsey will work with Large Load customers, including data centers, utilities, and other stakeholders to develop a framework expected to identify short- and mid-term solutions to interconnection queue issues in early 2026. The Electric Reliability Council of Texas announced a series of strategic organizational changes designed to accelerate innovation, strengthen grid reliability, and support the unprecedented growth in electric demand across Texas, with two new ERCOT organizations launching in January 2026.

articles.title: U.S. Electricity Demand Hits Record Peaks Amid Data Center Growth
articles.summary: Electricity demand has been rising steadily since 2020, with U.S. electricity demand growing about 1.7% annually between 2020 and 2025 compared with 0.1% annual growth between 2005 and 2019, driven primarily by electricity use in data centers and continued expansion of industrial electrification. Dozens of utilities received data center requests for at least 700 gigawatts of power connection development in 2025, which is more than the 477 GW in electricity that the United States consumed in all of 2023.

articles.title: Senators Introduce REWIRE Act to Modernize U.S. Electric Grid
articles.summary: Senators Dave McCormick and Peter Welch introduced the Reconductoring Existing Wires for Infrastructure Reliability and Expansion (REWIRE) Act of 2026, new bipartisan legislation to modernize the nation's electric grid and meet America's growing energy demand, which cuts permitting delays, incentivizes advanced transmission upgrades, and accelerates deployment of innovative grid technologies.
articles.sourceName: U.S. Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee

articles.title: Trump Administration Sends 15-Point Peace Plan to Iran via Pakistan
articles.summary: The U.S. has drafted and delivered a 15-point ceasefire proposal to Iran through Pakistani intermediaries, marking an escalation in diplomatic efforts to end the month-long war. The plan includes provisions for a 30-day ceasefire, dismantling of Iran's nuclear facilities, and reopening of the Strait of Hormuz, though Iran has rejected the proposal and counter-offered its own five-point plan demanding war reparations and sovereignty over the crucial waterway.

articles.title: Airport Crisis Worsens as TSA Faces Massive Staffing Shortages
articles.summary: Wait times at major U.S. airports are exceeding four hours amid a Department of Homeland Security shutdown, with TSA officers calling out at rates of 40-50% according to TSA Deputy Administrator Ha Nguyen McNeill. The shortages have prompted the deployment of ICE agents to 14 airports to assist during the crisis, while lawmakers and Trump officials remain gridlocked over DHS funding.

articles.title: Meta and Google Found Liable in Landmark Social Media Addiction Trial
articles.summary: A jury found Meta and Google liable on all charges in the first-ever jury trial over whether tech giants should be held accountable for social media addiction. The verdict marks a historic milestone in holding major technology platforms accountable for their role in digital addiction among users.

articles.title: Stock Markets Rally on Iran Peace Talk Optimism Despite Rejection
articles.summary: Stocks jumped on Wednesday as oil prices pulled back and traders hoped the U.S. and Iran could reach an agreement for a ceasefire, with the Dow Jones gaining 305.43 points (0.66%), the S&P 500 rising 0.54%, and the Nasdaq advancing 0.77%. The market optimism reflected investor hopes for a resolution to the month-long conflict despite Iran's rejection of the peace proposal.

articles.title: Thousands of Marines Deploy to Middle East as Trump Claims War Victory
articles.summary: Thousands of Marines are being sent to the Middle East, yet Trump says he's 'winding down' military efforts. The troop deployment totaling roughly 5,000 Marines and thousands of sailors is framed as giving the administration maximum flexibility in its approach to the Iran conflict.

articles.title: New Archbishop of Canterbury Installed as First Female Church Leader
articles.summary: A new archbishop of Canterbury has been installed in a historic ceremony, with Sarah Mullally being the 106th person to hold the job and the first woman. The appointment marks a significant milestone in the Anglican Communion's history.

articles.title: Freshwater Supplies Becoming Increasingly Salty Due to Human Activity
articles.summary: Around the world, the planet's freshwater is getting saltier because of people, as for decades, salting roads, fertilizer run-off and evaporation driven by human-caused climate change have increased the salinity of lakes, rivers and groundwater. Scientists are sounding alarms about the threat this poses to aquatic life and drinking water supplies.

articles.title: Supercomputers Solve 50-Year Mystery About Red Giant Stars
articles.summary: Astronomers have finally cracked a decades-old mystery about how material from deep interiors of red giant stars makes its way to the surface. Using cutting-edge supercomputer simulations, researchers discovered that stellar rotation plays a key role in this process.

articles.title: Scientists Identify Genetic Key to Fatty Liver Disease
articles.summary: Researchers identified microRNA-93 as a key genetic driver of fatty liver disease and discovered that vitamin B3 can effectively shut it down. This finding suggests a safe, widely available vitamin could become a powerful new treatment option.

articles.title: Arctic Rhinoceros Discovery Reshapes Understanding of Ancient Animal Distribution
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered a new species of rhinoceros in the Canadian High Arctic, revealing that rhinos once lived far farther north than expected. The fossil, dating back 23 million years, is unusually complete and has helped reshape ideas about prehistoric animal ranges.

articles.title: Webb Telescope Discovers Impossible Atmosphere on Ancient Super Earth
articles.summary: Astronomers have uncovered surprising evidence of a thick atmosphere surrounding TOI-561 b, a scorching, fast-orbiting rocky planet once thought too extreme to hold onto any gas. The discovery challenges assumptions about which planets can retain atmospheres.

articles.title: Beavers Emerge as Unexpected Climate Heroes
articles.summary: New research suggests that beavers could play a powerful role in fighting climate change. By building dams and transforming streams into wetlands, these industrious animals dramatically reshape how carbon moves through ecosystems and can trap large amounts of carbon.

articles.title: Ecosystems Slowing Down Despite Accelerating Climate Change
articles.summary: A massive global study shows that species turnover has slowed by about one-third since the 1970s, despite accelerating global warming. The unexpected deceleration suggests that biodiversity loss may be stalling nature's self-renewing machinery.

articles.title: World's First Quantum Battery Demonstrated in Australia
articles.summary: Scientists in Australia have demonstrated a prototype quantum battery that could revolutionize energy storage. By harnessing quantum effects, it can absorb energy in a rapid "super absorption" event, enabling much faster charging than conventional batteries.
